meta.title: Angular Pipes Guide – Built-in Pipes, Custom Pipes & Chaining (2026)
meta.description: Learn how to use Angular pipes to transform data directly in your HTML templates. Master DatePipe, CurrencyPipe, and how to build custom transformation logic.

content.value: Pipes are simple functions designed to accept an input value and transform it into a desired output for display in the UI. As a Technical Lead, you should use pipes to keep your component logic clean; instead of formatting a date or currency in your TypeScript class, you do it directly in the template. This follows the principle of separating 'Data' from 'Presentation'.
Angular comes with several built-in pipes for common data transformations. You use the 'pipe' operator (`|`) followed by the pipe name and any optional parameters (separated by colons).

{{ projectName | uppercase }}

{{ today | date:'mediumDate' }}

{{ price | currency:'INR' }}

{{ longDescription | slice:0:20 }}...
You can chain multiple pipes together to perform complex transformations in a single line. The output of the first pipe becomes the input for the next.
Pipes act as a middleman between your raw data and the final rendered view.

For high-performance applications like Revochamp, the `async` pipe is essential. It automatically subscribes to an **Observable** or **Promise** and returns the latest value it has emitted. Most importantly, it automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed to prevent memory leaks.

faq.question: What is a 'Pure' Pipe?
faq.answer: By default, pipes are 'pure'. This means Angular only executes the pipe when it detects a change to the input value (by reference). This makes them highly performant as they don't run on every change detection cycle.

faq.question: Can I create a custom pipe?
faq.answer: Absolutely. You can use 'ng generate pipe my-pipe'. A custom pipe is a class decorated with '@Pipe' that implements the 'PipeTransform' interface and its 'transform()' method.

faq.question: How do I pass multiple parameters to a pipe?
faq.answer: You separate them with colons. For example: '{{ val | slice:start:end }}'. Each colon adds a new argument to the transform function.

quiz.question: Which operator is used to apply a pipe in an Angular template?
Which pipe is used to handle Observables without manual subscriptions?
What happens when you chain pipes like '{{ val | pipeA | pipeB }}'?
options: The output of pipeA is passed as input to pipeB
It results in a syntax error
